MCL 722.27a(7) provides specific remedies for the wrongful denial of parenting time: | > "If a child's parent . . . has been wrongfully denied parenting time . . . the court shall do 1 or more of the following: (a) Grant additional parenting time to compensate for the parenting time wrongfully denied." | 79. The statute further authorizes the court to modify the custody or parenting time order, hold the offending party in contempt, and award actual costs and attorney fees. MCL 722.27a(7)(b)-(d).

Kramer*, 455 U.S. 745, 753 (1982), the Supreme Court held that the Due Process Clause of the Fourteenth Amendment protects the fundamental liberty interest of natural parents in the care, custody, and management of their child, and that before a state may sever this interest entirely, it must provide fundamentally fair procedures. Context Window: 46. The Michigan Child Custody Act establishes a clear and strong presumption that parenting time with both parents serves the best interests of the child (MCL 722.23). MCL 722.27a(1) provides: | > "Parenting time shall be granted in frequency, duration, and type reasonably calculated to promote a strong relationship between the child and the parent granted parenting time." | 47. This presumption reflects the Legislature's recognition — consistent with decades of social science research and constitutional law — that children benefit from maintaining strong, ongoing relationships with both parents.
